These results indicate that DE-2LS improves the exploitation capability of the RDEx-based search framework while preserving its speed advantage under the combined speed-accuracy scoring criterion. The source code of DE-2LS is available at https://github.com/ChauhanDikshit?tab=repositories.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DE-2LS, an enhanced variant of differential evolution tailored for constrained numerical optimization. Its late-stage local search refinement improves accuracy and computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges of balancing feasibility, convergence, and efficiency in numerical optimization under a fixed function-evaluation budget.
AI.method: DE-2LS extends the RDEx framework by incorporating a late-stage, lightweight coordinate-pattern local search as a polishing component. This is activated near the end of optimization runs and integrates feasibility-aware comparison rules.
AI.result: DE-2LS achieved higher performance than RDEx, displaying a 5.58% gain in U-score and outperforming three other algorithms in a four-algorithm comparison with the highest U-score and total rank.
AI.conclusion: Controlled late-stage refinement improves exploitation capabilities while maintaining computational efficiency, making DE-2LS a robust enhancement for constrained optimization tasks.

title: DiStash: A Disaggregated Multi-Stash Transactional Key-Value Store
comment: A shorter version of this paper appeared In the Seventeenth TPC Technology Conference on Performance Evaluation and Benchmarking, Pages 115 - 133, co-located with VLDB 2025, London, UK, September 1, 2025
summary: A stash is a storage medium such as Dynamic Random Access Memory (DRAM), Solid State Disk (SSD), Hard Disk Drive (HDD), or Non-Volatile Memory (NVM). This paper presents a disaggregated transactional key-value (KV) store, DiStash, that governs KVs cross pools of stash types. It enables an application to use a single transaction to read and write different copies of one or more key-value pair across the different pools of stashes. It simplifies the application logic by (a) preventing undesirable race conditions that may cause copies of data across different stash pools to reflect different values and/or (b) failures that may result in loss of key-value pairs. A configuration of DiStash may use a pool of stashes as either ephemeral or durable storage. The application dictates whether the content of its participating stashes are inclusive (replicated) or exclusive (tiered). We implement a DiStash by extending FoundationDB. We quantify the tradeoffs with its design decisions using microbenchmarks and eBay's production workload. We open source our implementation at https://github.com/ebay-USC/DiStash.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DiStash, a disaggregated transactional key-value store that supports multi-stash type transactions and mitigates data inconsistencies or failures.
AI.motivation: To address undesirable race conditions and data inconsistencies across different storage pools, simplifying the application logic in managing key-value pairs.
AI.method: The authors extended FoundationDB to implement DiStash, allowing transactions across different storage pools and offering configurations for ephemeral or durable usage, along with inclusive or exclusive storage options.
AI.result: The paper evaluates design trade-offs in DiStash using both microbenchmarks and a real-world workload from eBay to demonstrate its benefits.
AI.conclusion: DiStash simplifies transactional operations across diverse storage media, improving reliability and consistency while being open-sourced for community use.

title: Optimizing Teacher-Student Partitioning for Scalable Knowledge Distillation on HPC Systems
summary: Knowledge Distillation (KD) enables training smaller student models under the guidance of larger teacher models, and the widely adopted TRL library implements it. Yet, TRL treats both models symmetrically, missing opportunities to exploit their pronounced asymmetry in memory footprint, and communication requirements. This paper presents an HPC-aware methodology for KD that decouples teacher and student partitioning efficiently. Our approach achieves up to 67% higher samples-per-second than TRL by avoiding unnecessary teacher-model data structures and selecting the best split strategy. We combine vertical and horizontal partitioning of models, deriving an analytical expression that identifies the existence of inflection points between splitting regimes. These results showed that exploiting teacher--student asymmetry through topology-aware parallelism notably accelerated GKD training on production HPC clusters at our company

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an HPC-aware methodology for Knowledge Distillation (KD), focusing on exploiting asymmetries between teacher and student models, and achieves up to 67% faster performance than existing TRL methods.
AI.motivation: TRL library treats teacher and student models symmetrically, missing opportunities to optimize memory and communication based on their differing requirements.
AI.method: The paper proposes efficient decoupling of teacher and student partitioning through topology-aware parallelism, combining vertical and horizontal model partitioning, backed by analytical expressions for splitting regimes.
AI.result: The approach yielded up to 67% higher samples-per-second compared to TRL by optimizing partitioning and avoiding redundant data structures.
AI.conclusion: Exploiting teacher-student asymmetry and optimizing topology-aware model partitioning significantly accelerates KD training in high-performance computing environments.

title: The quantum instrument monad
comment: 28 pages. Independent work by Booth, Leichtle, Rice and Worrall develops a closely related construction in the Heisenberg picture. The two works provide complementary Schrödinger- and Heisenberg-picture formulations
summary:
Monads are a ubiquitous structure in functional programming used for modelling computational effects. For example, the state monad models the effect of a computation interacting with a memory system. Here we introduce the quantum instrument monad $\mathcal{I}_\mathcal{A}$, which models the effect of a computation interacting with a quantum system with algebra of observables $\mathcal{A}$. It can be thought of as a noncommutative generalization of the state monad.
  We construct this quantum instrument monad in two versions: a finitary version on the category of sets and a measure-theoretic version on the category of measurable spaces (the latter under the assumption that $\mathcal{A}$ is a type I von Neumann algebra with separable predual). Both versions are strong monads. The construction of the measure-theoretic version is based on a new notion of integral of a quantum-operation-valued function against a state-valued measure.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a quantum instrument monad, extending functional programming tools to model computations interacting with quantum systems.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between computational modeling in classical functional programming and quantum systems by generalizing the state monad to quantum systems.
AI.method: The authors construct two versions of the quantum instrument monad: a finitary version on sets and a measure-theoretic version using von Neumann algebras and state-valued measures.
AI.result: Both versions of the constructed monad are strong monads, with the measure-theoretic version leveraging a novel quantum-operation-valued integral.
AI.conclusion: The quantum instrument monad enables modeling computational effects in quantum systems, expanding the theory of monads to the quantum domain.

title: Heterogeneous synaptic motifs bridge microscale structure and macroscale nonlinear dynamics
summary: Recent breakthroughs in synaptic-resolution network connectomics have revealed that brain circuits feature fine-scale structural connectivity, such as pairs of correlated synaptic couplings known as second-order motifs. Large-scale recordings of neuronal activity in networks containing nonlinear neurons reveal macroscopic heterogeneous population dynamics throughout the brain. These findings rekindle the inquiry into this intriguing question: Can microscale synaptic structures contribute to macroscopic heterogeneous dynamics and computations in ways that canonical brain circuit models cannot? To answer this question, we create random RNNs with various cell types, nonlinear non-negative neural responses, and arbitrary marginal and second-order correlated synaptic statistics. We derive mean-field low-rank equations for P-population networks in which the pre- and postsynaptic neuronal population identities determine the synaptic and motif strengths. Our framework requires 2P latent dynamic variables with P variables describing mean population activity and P variables capturing within-population variability. Theoretical and simulational results demonstrate that chain motifs induce correlations in synaptic variability, enabling microscopic fluctuations to be integrated and influence mesoscopic mean population dynamics. We apply this framework to reverse engineer network connectivity that recapitulates the heterogeneous activity across the population in the mouse primary visual cortex. By bridging the gap between synaptic organization and nonlinear heterogeneous population dynamics, our results offer a principled approach and testable predictions regarding the relationship between fine-scale connectivity, heterogeneous dynamics, and functional computations.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the impact of microscale synaptic structures on macroscopic heterogeneous dynamics and computations in brain circuits, introducing a novel framework to model such interactions.
AI.motivation: To explore whether fine-scale synaptic connectivity, like second-order motifs, contributes to macroscopic heterogeneous brain dynamics and computations in ways that traditional models fail to address.
AI.method: The authors create random recurrent neural networks (RNNs) with nonlinear neural responses and correlated synaptic statistics, deriving mean-field equations for population dynamics influenced by synaptic motifs.
AI.result: The study demonstrates that chain motifs can induce variability correlations, allowing microscopic synaptic fluctuations to shape mesoscopic population dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide insights into how fine-scale connectivity can influence brain dynamics and computational functions, with applications in modeling activity in areas like the mouse visual cortex.

title: A Survey of Automated Presentation Coaching: Systems, Methods, and Open Challenges
comment: accepted into the BEA 2026 workshop at ACL
summary: Automated coaching for oral presentations sits at the intersection of computer-assisted pronunciation training (CAPT), prosody modeling, and speech synthesis, yet no prior work has systematically surveyed and compared existing systems along these dimensions. This survey reviews and categorizes automated presentation coaching systems, spanning pronunciation tutors, fluency and prosody coaches, multimodal trainers, and conference Q&A practice tools. We introduce a five-dimensional task taxonomy - covering segmental pronunciation, lexical stress, suprasegmental prosody, pacing, and content faithfulness - and explicitly map surveyed systems onto it to reveal coverage gaps. We further review the core technical methods these systems employ: TTS-based exemplar generation and diagnostic methods for pronunciation, prosody, and fluency assessment. Key open challenges include the scarcity of annotated presentation corpora, achieving accent-fair feedback across diverse L1 backgrounds, and delivering low-latency diagnostics for real-time rehearsal.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews and categorizes automated systems for oral presentation coaching, introduces a five-dimensional task taxonomy, and highlights challenges such as data scarcity and accent fairness.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of a systematic survey and comparison of existing automated coaching systems for oral presentations in terms of key dimensions like pronunciation and prosody.
AI.method: The authors categorize existing systems into pronunciation tutors, fluency and prosody coaches, multimodal trainers, and Q&A practice tools, and map them onto a five-dimensional taxonomy while reviewing their technical methods.
AI.result: Systematic mapping of existing systems revealed coverage gaps in the five-dimensional taxonomy, and challenges such as the need for annotated datasets and fair feedback mechanisms were identified.
AI.conclusion: There is a need for more robust, accent-fair, and real-time-capable systems in automated presentation coaching, alongside improved data resources and technical innovation.

title: Formal Grammars in Business Process Management: A Systematic Literature Review
summary: Business Process Management (BPM) is concerned with the systematic design, execution, monitoring, and improvement of business processes. Formal grammars have emerged as a particularly fruitful formalism for BPM, offering generative, declarative, and analytical capabilities that are uniquely well-suited to process-oriented concerns. This paper presents a systematic literature review of 34 primary studies at the intersection of formal grammars and BPM. We identify seven research streams: (i) process grammars for organizational process design; (ii) process modeling languages evaluated as grammars under the Bunge-Wand-Weber ontological framework; (iii) production-rule grammars for process structural specification and variant management; (iv) attribute grammars for the declarative specification and distributed execution of workflows; (v) graph grammars for the transformation, generation, and semantic analysis of process models; (vi) grammatical inference for process mining and discovery; and (vii) process algebras as grammar-like compositional frameworks for behavioral specification and verification. For each stream, we synthesize contributions, formalisms employed, and limitations. The review reveals that formal grammars have influenced BPM across every lifecycle phase (from organizational design to formal verification and data-driven discovery) yet the seven streams have developed largely in parallel, without cross-stream synthesis. We identify five corpus-grounded open challenges and argue that a deeper, unified exploitation of grammatical theory holds significant promise for advancing the state of the art in BPM.

AI.tldr: The paper reviews 34 studies linking formal grammars and BPM, identifying seven main research streams and highlighting gaps and challenges while proposing unification for progress.
AI.motivation: To explore how formal grammars contribute to various aspects of Business Process Management (BPM) and identify gaps or opportunities for further research.
AI.method: The authors conduct a systematic literature review of 34 primary studies focused on the intersection of formal grammars and BPM.
AI.result: Seven research streams are identified, each focusing on different applications of formal grammars in BPM. A lack of cross-stream integration and five open challenges are highlighted.
AI.conclusion: Formal grammars have substantial influence across BPM lifecycle phases but lack holistic integration. A unified exploration of grammatical theory could significantly advance BPM research.

title: Spacecraft Fiducial Marker for Autonomous Rendezvous, Proximity Operations, and Docking
summary: Robotic operations in space are challenging due to the harsh environment and the high cost of failure. Fiducial markers provide visual references that aid autonomous rendezvous, proximity operations, and docking for space robots. However, existing fiducial markers are mostly single-scale and largely designed for terrestrial robotics. Such markers leave the camera's field of view at close range, precisely during the proximity and docking phases where reliable tracking is most critical. This paper presents AstraTag, a fiducial marker designed for autonomous on-orbit robotic operations. The marker template is based on a square Spidron pattern whose recursive, self-similar structure enables detection across multiple spatial scales. Marker identification uses a 48-bit signature derived from triangular sub-regions of the template and encoded with a Generalised Reed-Solomon (GRS) code. The detection pipeline performs contour-based quadrilateral localisation, perspective normalisation, and signature matching against a pre-computed dictionary. To handle markers affixed to curved spacecraft surfaces, it incorporates a Thin-Plate Spline (TPS) re-warp fallback that exploits the marker's internal rectangular borders as additional geometric correspondences. We benchmark AstraTag against three-layer Fractal ArUco and AprilTag on spacecraft mockups with flat and curved surfaces. On curved surfaces, AstraTag achieves a higher detection rate than both baselines, offering a robust recursive-marker option for space robotics.

AI.tldr: AstraTag is a new fiducial marker designed for robotic operations in space, with a self-similar structure enabling multi-scale detection and robust performance on flat and curved surfaces.
AI.motivation: Space robotic operations require reliable vision-based aids due to the harsh environment and high failure costs, but current fiducial markers fail in critical scenarios like proximity and docking.
AI.method: The AstraTag marker uses a recursive Spidron pattern, 48-bit signature with Reed-Solomon coding, contour-based detection, TPS re-warp for curved surfaces, and a signature-matching pipeline.
AI.result: AstraTag outperforms Fractal ArUco and AprilTag in detection rates, particularly on curved surfaces of spacecraft mockups.
AI.conclusion: AstraTag presents a robust and effective fiducial marker solution for autonomous robotic operations in space.

title: Internalizing the Future: A Unified Agentic Training Paradigm for World Model Planning
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents have demonstrated strong capability in sequential decision-making, yet they remains fundamentally reactive in long-horizon tasks. Unlike humans who employ "what-if" reasoning to evaluate potential plans before commitment, standard agents lack an internal world model to simulate future outcomes. Therefore, we propose to internalize future-aware planning by training a single autoregressive model to verbalize both a prospective state rollout and a plan-conditioned success estimate-a textual analogue of the Q-value. Crucially, we identify a format-capability gap: simply fine-tuning agents on look-ahead traces during post-training leads to superficial mimicry of foresight without genuine predictive grounding. To bridge this gap, we introduce a three-stage training paradigm: (i) World Model Agentic Mid-Training (WM-AMT) to inject latent predictive capabilities into the policy; (ii) Format-Eliciting SFT (FE-SFT) to structure this injected capability; and (iii) Foresight-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning (FC-RL) to refine the calibration and utility of the generated simulations. Evaluated on search and mathematical reasoning tasks, our approach consistently outperforms other training baselines. Our results demonstrate that effective internal world modeling in LLM agents requires a capability-first training pipeline to achieve grounded and calibrated foresight.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a method to enhance large language model agents by incorporating predictive foresight through a three-stage training pipeline, improving their performance in long-horizon decision-making tasks.
AI.motivation: Large language models excel at sequential decision-making but are limited by their reactive nature in long-horizon tasks, lacking foresight mechanisms akin to human 'what-if' reasoning.
AI.method: The paper proposes a three-stage training process: (i) WM-AMT to inject latent predictive capabilities, (ii) FE-SFT to structure these capabilities, and (iii) FC-RL to refine and calibrate these predictive abilities.
AI.result: The enhanced internal world modeling approach results in superior performance on tasks such as search and mathematical reasoning, compared to baseline models.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating foresight through a capability-first training pipeline is essential to enable grounded and calibrated predictive reasoning in large language model agents.

title: Fine-tuning a multimodal large language model for clinician-grade autism behavioral scoring from short home videos
summary: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) affects 1 in 31 US children, yet median age at diagnosis exceeds four years. Artificial intelligence pipelines that provide quantified diagnosis using easy to access observational data (e.g., home videos) could help with earlier diagnosis, and timely delivery of early treatments. We fine-tuned Gemini 2.5 Pro on 400 clinician-rated home videos with low-rank adaptation, training only on 30 behavioral features previously validated to produce reliable predictions when passed to various ML models. On 99 held-out children (49 ASD, 50 neurotypical), inter-rater reliability with clinicians (per-feature weighted Cohen's kappa) improved by 40% (p<0.001), with 27 of 28 evaluable features improving. As an emergent zero-shot capability, direct ASD diagnosis F1 improved by 53% (p<0.001), matching or exceeding clinician outcomes. Classifier-assisted pipelines using fine-tuned LLM-derived behavioral features matched clinician-scored inputs across all tested pathways and achieved 77% accuracy (95% CI: 68-85%) and an AUC of 86% (95% CI: 78-92%). Fine-tuned multimodal LLMs can serve as scalable behavioral feature extractors for use in autism assessment and diagnosis.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a fine-tuned AI pipeline using home video data for earlier autism diagnosis, achieving high precision and diagnostic performance comparable to clinicians.
AI.motivation: To address the delayed diagnosis of autism in children by leveraging artificial intelligence to analyze accessible observational data, thereby enabling earlier interventions and treatment.
AI.method: The authors fine-tuned a multimodal large language model (Gemini 2.5 Pro) on 400 clinician-rated home videos using low-rank adaptation, focusing on 30 validated behavioral features. They tested their model on 99 independent children.
AI.result: The AI model showed a 40% improvement in inter-rater reliability with clinicians and a 53% improvement in diagnostic F1 score, reaching clinician-level prediction accuracy with 77% performance and an AUC of 86%.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuned large language models can serve as scalable tools for autism diagnosis using readily available observational data, delivering performance comparable to clinicians.

title: Adversarial Contamination Meets Hard Thresholding: An Iterative Algorithm with Signal Adaptivity and Minimax Optimality
summary: Pervasive data contamination -- stemming from measurement errors, outliers, or adversarial corruption -- has motivated the development of robust statistical methods. In this context, we propose a two-stage Adversarial Contamination-resistant Iterative Hard Thresholding (AC-IHT) algorithm for high-dimensional regression with contamination. Our nonconvex algorithm achieves minimax near-optimal (up to logarithmic terms) estimation by iteratively updating the coefficient vector and the contamination vector with different thresholding scales. We further demonstrate that our AC-IHT estimator is signal-adaptive: under proper signal conditions, it adaptively attains a sharper estimation rate and more accurate support recovery. Moreover, it enjoys the strong oracle property, laying a theoretical foundation for asymptotic inference. Numerical experiments confirm its superior finite-sample performance. Finally, we discuss theoretical extensions of the proposed procedure to generalized linear models and to heavy-tailed noise settings.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the AC-IHT algorithm for robust high-dimensional regression, achieving strong theoretical and practical performance against data contamination.
AI.motivation: To address pervasive data contamination due to measurement errors, outliers, or adversarial corruption in statistical methods.
AI.method: A two-stage Adversarial Contamination-resistant Iterative Hard Thresholding (AC-IHT) algorithm that iteratively updates coefficient and contamination vectors using different thresholding scales.
AI.result: The AC-IHT algorithm achieves minimax near-optimal estimation, adapts to signal-specific conditions for better estimation and support recovery, and exhibits strong numerical performance.
AI.conclusion: The AC-IHT algorithm provides a robust, theoretically sound approach for contaminated high-dimensional regression and can be extended to generalized linear models and heavy-tailed noise settings.

title: Unified Zero-Shot Time Series Forecasting: A Darts Foundation
summary: Since its initial release in 2020, Darts has become a widely used open-source Python library for time series analysis. A series of foundation models have recently claimed accuracy improvements in zero-shot forecasting, promising a paradigm shift from training custom models to harnessing pre-trained general-purpose forecasters. Foundation models, however, are often released as isolated packages with fragmented interfaces and limited interoperability with common tooling, making joint evaluation and integration within complete pipelines difficult. In Darts, we developed a unified $\texttt{FoundationModel}$ class collection (Chronos-2, TimesFM 2.5, TiRex, PatchTST-FM) that provides standardized, full-cycle forecasting interfaces with minimal external dependencies for integrating foundation models into the ecosystem. Existing Darts pipelines can now use foundation models with only a name change; new pipelines can use them for zero-shot or fine-tuned forecasting, uncertainty estimation, and backtesting, combined with data processing and evaluation tooling, all within a unified framework.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a unified class in the Darts library that integrates foundation models for time series analysis seamlessly.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses challenges in integrating foundation models (released as isolated packages) for time series forecasting into end-to-end pipelines effectively.
AI.method: Develop a standardized class ($\texttt{FoundationModel}$) within the Darts library to integrate models like Chronos-2 and PatchTST-FM with minimal dependencies.
AI.result: The new functionality allows users to adopt foundation models in their Darts pipelines via a unified framework for zero-shot, fine-tuned forecasting, uncertainty estimation, and backtesting.
AI.conclusion: By providing standardized interfaces, the Darts library significantly simplifies the adoption and interoperability of foundation models in time series analysis workflows.

title: AI-Driven Synthesis for High-Tech System Design: Automating Innovation
summary: This article addresses the combinatorial complexity inherent in modern high-tech system design by presenting automation-in-design (AiD) as a transformative paradigm. We propose computational design synthesis (CDS), a framework utilising deep learning and generative AI to automate the creation of novel systems. Two case studies (e-drive system design and spatial dimensioning problem) serve as proof-points for this approach. The AI-driven methods used in the case studies represent a fundamental shift in engineering, advancing from simulation-based optimisation towards autonomous design with minimal human supervision.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces automation-in-design (AiD) using deep learning and generative AI for system design, demonstrated via two case studies.
AI.motivation: To address the complexity of high-tech system design and enable transformative changes in engineering.
AI.method: Utilization of computational design synthesis (CDS) leveraging deep learning and generative AI to automate system creation.
AI.result: Two case studies validate the proposed AI-driven methods, showcasing reduced human supervision in design processes.
AI.conclusion: The study signifies a paradigm shift in engineering, demonstrating the feasibility of autonomous design systems through AI-driven methodologies.

title: MMAO: A Metabolic Multi-Agent Optimizer with Endogenous Resource Allocation for Continuous and Discrete Optimization
summary: Traditional meta-heuristics often rely on fixed population sizes, manually chosen search scales, and externally attached parameter-control modules. This paper presents the \textit{Metabolic Multi-Agent Optimizer} (MMAO), a cross-domain optimization framework in which adaptation is derived endogenously from a private-public metabolic resource loop. Each agent carries internal energy, a continuous role state, motion or structural memory, and local search history, while the population shares a communal resource pool. Fitness improvements are converted into normalized metabolic gains through a robust progress scale and a recent success statistic; the same closed loop then regulates sensing intensity, search amplitude, role drift, branching, pruning, respawning, and elite reinvestment. In the continuous setting, MMAO uses energy-regulated symmetric zero-order probing and role-interpolated motion. In the discrete setting, the same control law is instantiated through structural sensing, local route improvement, guided perturbation, and energy-weighted edge reuse. The paper combines an implementation-faithful formulation with a reproducible experimental study on a CEC2017 subset (10D/30D, 20 seeds) and five TSPLIB instances (100 discrete runs in total). The current evidence supports MMAO primarily as a parameter-light, self-calibrating optimization framework whose main validated originality lies in metabolically endogenous resource allocation across heterogeneous search behaviors, rather than as a universally superior optimizer.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Metabolic Multi-Agent Optimizer (MMAO), an adaptive optimization framework using a metabolic resource-sharing approach for tuning search agents.
AI.motivation: Enhance optimization algorithms by addressing reliance on fixed configurations, manually tuned parameters, and external control systems.
AI.method: MMAO utilizes a metabolic loop linking agents' internal energy and a shared resource pool to adapt search behaviors automatically across both continuous and discrete optimization domains.
AI.result: Experiments show MMAO as a self-calibrating framework with improvements in resource allocation and heterogeneous search behavior, validated on CEC2017 benchmarks and TSPLIB instances.
AI.conclusion: MMAO demonstrates originality in metabolically-driven adaptation but does not outperform all optimizers universally. Its strength lies in its parameter-light nature and endogenous resource control mechanism.

title: RAMSES: Secure high-performance computing for sensitive data
summary: Traditionally, the architecture of high-performance computing (HPC) systems is tailored for speed, while highly secure computer systems must sacrifice speed for security. However, a wide range of scientific domains, such as the life sciences, call for a combination of performance and security to allow processing sensitive data at scale. Here, we present RAMSES (Research Accelerator for Modeling and Simulation with Enhanced Security), an HPC system designed from the ground up to deliver high performance within a robust security framework. RAMSES integrates hardware-based memory encryption of AMD processors with state-of-the-art file encryption from IBM Storage Scale and the Thales CipherTrust manager, establishing an HPC platform that ensures continuous encryption throughout the data life cycle - at rest, in transit, and in use - in compliance with major data protection standards (European General Data Protection Regulation, ISO/IEC 27001 certification, and Federal Information Processing Standards). In addition, we implemented advanced operating system hardening, a multi-layered security architecture, and mandatory multi-factor authentication to adapt the HPC environment to increased security demands. Benchmark results from the biomedical sector demonstrate that the performance impact of the secure environment is limited and that integration of the conflicting requirements speed and security can be achieved while preserving a coherent, flexible, and user-friendly system.

AI.tldr: RAMSES is a high-performance computing (HPC) system designed to integrate speed and robust security, particularly for domains requiring processing of sensitive data.
AI.motivation: To address the need for HPC systems that balance high speed and robust security, especially in scientific domains like life sciences where sensitive data is handled.
AI.method: RAMSES combines AMD hardware-based memory encryption, IBM Storage Scale's file encryption, and Thales CipherTrust for full data life-cycle security. It also incorporates OS hardening, multi-layered security, and mandatory multi-factor authentication.
AI.result: Benchmark results from the biomedical field show minimal performance trade-offs while ensuring robust security in processing sensitive data.
AI.conclusion: RAMSES effectively merges high performance and stringent security requirements, creating a secure yet user-friendly HPC environment suitable for sensitive data processing.

title: Causal Connections: Leveraging Multilingual Fine-Tuning for Financial QA@FinCausal 2026
summary: This paper describes team HSA_CORAL's submission to the FinCausal 2026 shared task on extracting cause-effect relations from financial narratives via extractive question answering in English and Spanish. We compare three modeling families: (i) encoder-only token tagging with multilingual BERT, (ii) encoder-decoder generation with multilingual BART, and (iii) decoder-only LLMs (Llama 3.1 and GPT variants) using prompt refinement, few-shot demonstrations, and supervised fine-tuning. Across settings, prompting and few-shot examples yield competitive performance, while supervised fine-tuning provides the largest gains. Our best system, GPT-4.1 Mini fine-tuned on combined English and Spanish training data, achieves a tied highest score on the English subtask (score 4.8140) and ranks third on Spanish (score 4.7753) under the shared task's LLM-as-a-judge metric. Overall, the results highlight the value of task-specific adaptation and multilingual fine-tuning for cross-lingual transfer in financial causality QA.

AI.tldr: This paper discusses team HSA_CORAL's approach to FinCausal 2026, employing various modeling techniques for extracting cause-effect relations from financial texts in English and Spanish.
AI.motivation: To advance the extraction of causal relationships in multilingual financial narratives using state-of-the-art question-answering methods.
AI.method: The team compared three modeling families—encoder-only (multilingual BERT), encoder-decoder (multilingual BART), and decoder-only LLMs (Llama 3.1 and GPT)—using techniques like prompting, few-shot learning, and fine-tuning.
AI.result: The best-performing system, GPT-4.1 Mini fine-tuned on English and Spanish data, scored highest for the English subtask (4.8140) and third for the Spanish subtask (4.7753).
AI.conclusion: Task-specific adaptation and multilingual fine-tuning are essential strategies for enhancing cross-lingual performance in financial causality extraction tasks.

title: Recall Before Rerank: Benchmarking Deep Learning Models for Large-Scale Code-to-Code Retrieval
summary: Semantic code search and clone detection are essential for software development, maintenance, and reuse. This paper evaluates the effectiveness, efficiency, and scalability of contemporary deep learning models for first-stage recall in large-scale code-to-code search engines. Benchmarking across multiple programming languages and datasets reveals critical limits in the precision and scalability of these models on Terabyte-scale source-code collections. We present LLM-based code normalisation and query-rewriting schemes that yield significant gains in precision for lower-performing models. Our results question the sustainability of resource-constrained deployment and the assumed robustness of current code-specialised LLMs across datasets. We conclude with actionable insights for building scalable, efficient code-retrieval systems.

AI.tldr: The paper assesses deep learning models for large-scale code-to-code search, highlighting challenges in precision and scalability, and proposing LLM-based improvements.
AI.motivation: To improve the effectiveness and scalability of semantic code search and clone detection for large-scale software applications.
AI.method: Evaluated deep learning models on multilingual datasets, identifying limitations and introducing LLM-based code normalisation/query-rewriting techniques.
AI.result: Achieved significant precision improvements for weaker models but raised concerns about the scalability and dataset robustness of current code-specialised LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Offered practical guidance for creating scalable and efficient code-retrieval systems while questioning the sustainability of current model approaches.

title: SceneBot: Contact-Prompted General Humanoid Whole Body Tracking with Scene-Interaction
summary: Current humanoid reinforcement-learning policies excel at free-space motions but struggle with contact-rich tasks, as pure kinematic tracking cannot resolve the physical ambiguities of interacting with objects and uneven terrain. To address this, we introduce SceneBot, a unified motion-tracking framework capable of handling freespace locomotion, terrain traversal, and whole-body manipulation. SceneBot conditions a single policy on both reference motions and per-link contact labels, explicitly defining expected environmental interactions. To overcome the lack of annotated interaction data, we propose a hindsight scene reconstruction approach that infers scene-interaction graphs from retargeted human motion. Trained on 7.5 hours of this reconstructed, contact-rich data, SceneBot successfully generalizes to unseen motions and environments. Our results demonstrate that SceneBot is the first general framework to seamlessly unify free-space and contact-rich behaviors executing complex, long-horizon tasks like carrying a box upstairs and establishing contact conditioning as a powerful interface for humanoid control. All code and data will be open-sourced. More demos and information are available at: https://ericcsr.github.io/scenebot/

AI.tldr: SceneBot is a humanoid reinforcement-learning framework that excels in free-space and contact-rich tasks using a policy conditioned on both reference motions and contact labels.
AI.motivation: Humanoid reinforcement-learning policies are effective in free-space motions but fail in contact-rich tasks, which require resolving environmental interactions and physical ambiguities.
AI.method: SceneBot uses a unified motion-tracking framework that incorporates reference motions and per-link contact labels. It employs a hindsight scene reconstruction approach to infer scene-interaction data from retargeted human motion.
AI.result: Trained on 7.5 hours of reconstructed interaction data, SceneBot generalizes well to unseen environments and motions, executing complex tasks like carrying a box upstairs.
AI.conclusion: SceneBot is the first general framework that unifies free-space and contact-rich behaviors, making robust and adaptive humanoid control viable. Code and data will be open-sourced.

title: Odyssey: Constructing Verifiable Local Truth-Preserving Foundation Models
summary: We introduce a categorical framework called ODYSSEY for constructing verifiable, local truth-preserving foundation models as compositions of foundries: building-block architectural components that specify a cover of local contexts, local representation families, restriction maps, gluing rules, obstruction policies, update obligations, and human-facing views. A foundry is an organized sheaf of knowledge that carries within it an argumentation component. Concrete foundries are built from generic foundries such as evidence/argument, operational decision, institutional/financial, market meaning, scientific challenge, research-program, assistant-build, and evaluation-harness foundries. Universal Foundry Learning (UFL) formalizes foundry construction as a composition of left and right Kan extensions, with left Kan extension rolling local artifacts into candidate foundries and right Kan extension enforcing the restriction, gluing, obstruction, and argumentation conditions required for promotion. Foundry SQL (FSQL) is a small typed query surface for slicing maintained foundry artifacts that uses TICKET (Topos Integration using Causal Kan Extension Transformers) certification for admitting external or pre-built models into durable ODYSSEY state. ODYSSEY is fully implemented and tested across a wide spectrum of concrete foundries, showing that the same categorical machinery supports domain construction, artifact replay, sheaf diagnostics, grounded Toulmin/local-LLM scrutiny, residual-obstruction ledgers, and optimized TICKET-compatible causal-claim extraction across heterogeneous sources. This paper is to be presented as a 2.5 hour tutorial at ICML 2026. The tutorial home page is at https://bit.ly/4ajS0nA.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ODYSSEY, a framework using categorical methods to construct verifiable, truth-preserving models, integrating a variety of knowledge contexts.
AI.motivation: To create foundational models that are local truth-preserving and verifiable, addressing complex real-world domains through modular and extensible methodologies.
AI.method: The ODYSSEY framework utilizes foundries with specific components (e.g., restriction maps, gluing rules, argumentation) and constructs them via left and right Kan extensions for universal foundry learning. Foundry SQL ensures artifact management and compatibility.
AI.result: ODYSSEY has been fully implemented and tested on diverse domains, supporting tasks like domain construction, diagnostics, argument scrutiny, and causal claim extraction.
AI.conclusion: The ODYSSEY framework effectively unifies various theoretical and practical tools to handle heterogeneous knowledge sources in a verifiable and structured manner.

title: SelectAnyTree: A Promptable Instance Segmentation Model for 3D Forest LiDAR Point Clouds
summary: Automated instance segmentation of forest LiDAR point clouds is increasingly critical as forest monitoring moves toward scalable, detailed, 3D measurement. Yet, progress is constrained by label scarcity for tree instances; a single hectare can hold millions of points and hundreds of overlapping, complex crowns, making manual annotation from scratch with raw data laborious and error-prone. Annotations are often corrected from automatic pre-segmentations, but remain costly as these provide no interactive or AI-assisted refinement. Inspired by the promptable paradigm of foundation segmentation models, we propose SelectAnyTree, a promptable instance segmentation model that delineates any individual tree in a 3D forest point cloud from a few clicks. It introduces two key components: Click-to-query prompt encoder and Canopy Height Model (CHM)-guided first prompt. The former turns each click into a single content query, encoding its 3D position and positive/negative polarity together with a pooled local backbone feature. The latter provides treetops as a geometry- and ecologically guided first prompt without any user input. The resulting prompt query is then decoded into one tree mask by a state-space query decoder to efficiently capture long-range context in large-scale forest scenes with linear-time complexity. We evaluate SelectAnyTree in interactive and instance-level settings across seven diverse forest regions and an independent held-out test dataset, demonstrating strong generalization beyond the training domains. It segments a target tree to 78.2 Intersection over Union (IoU) from a single click, 24.8 points above the strongest promptable baseline, and reaches every accuracy target with the fewest clicks, while using far fewer parameters and less inference time than prior promptable models. The source code is available at https://github.com/thanhhff/SelectAnyTree.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SelectAnyTree, a promptable instance segmentation model designed to segment individual trees in 3D forest LiDAR point clouds, significantly improving efficiency and accuracy compared to prior methods.
AI.motivation: The research addresses the challenge of instance segmentation in forest LiDAR point clouds due to the scarcity of labeled data, high complexity of overlapping trees, and labor-intensive manual annotations.
AI.method: The proposed SelectAnyTree uses a Click-to-query prompt encoder for interactive tree instance segmentation and integrates a Canopy Height Model-guided first prompt for initialization. It employs a state-space query decoder for efficient segmentation in large-scale forest scenes.
AI.result: SelectAnyTree achieves 78.2 IoU from a single click, surpassing other promptable models by 24.8 points, and reaches accuracy targets with fewer clicks, parameters, and inference time.
AI.conclusion: SelectAnyTree demonstrates robust performance and generalization across diverse forest datasets, offering a scalable, efficient solution for 3D forest monitoring. The model code is publicly available.

title: Local Fokker--Planck Geometry for Score Estimation: Heat-Ball Mean-Value Representations and Exact High-Dimensional Sampling
summary: Score-based generative models and Langevin samplers rely on estimating the score function $\nabla_x\log p_t(x)$ of a forward diffusion. Classically this is tractable when the drift is linear: the marginal density is Gaussian and the score is a global conditional expectation. For a general nonlinear, state-dependent drift the marginal density has no closed form, and existing methods--denoising score matching and global Fokker--Planck residual penalties--resort to global averaging that inflates estimation error in low-density regions precisely where accuracy is most critical. We address this by developing a local Fokker--Planck geometric framework that replaces global conditioning with local parabolic averaging. Our approach rests on three contributions. First, a time change to the cumulative-variance coordinate reduces the variable-coefficient Fokker--Planck equation to a standard inhomogeneous heat equation, on which we extend Evans' classical heat-ball monotonicity method to derive exact local mean-value representations for the score $\nabla_x\log p$ together with the density, log-density, and entropy density; local well-posedness is established under an explicit dimension-dependent drift budget. Second, for high-dimensional Monte Carlo evaluation of the resulting heat-ball integrals, we introduce the $κ$-measure and derive its exact factorized sampler with unit per-sample weight, $χ^2_2$ radial concentration. Third, the $r\to0$ limit of the heat-ball residual recovers the pointwise Fokker--Planck residual, showing that the local framework is a one-parameter generalization of global FP-residual methods, and that the DSM population minimizer is feasible for the heat-ball constraint at every scale. We validate the framework on 2D structured data on 256-dimensional MNIST, and on a dedicated sampler study confirming the concentration laws.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a local geometric framework for score estimation in diffusion processes, improving accuracy in low-density areas compared to traditional methods.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the challenges posed by existing score-estimation methods which inflate errors in low-density regions, particularly for nonlinear, state-dependent drifts where marginal densities have no closed form.
AI.method: A local Fokker–Planck framework is proposed based on time changes, local parabolic averaging, and exact local mean-value representations. This involves transforming the Fokker–Planck equation to a heat equation, creating a $κ$-measure for efficient high-dimensional evaluation, and generalizing the global FP-residual approach.
AI.result: The framework theoretically and empirically improves accuracy, particularly under high-dimensions, as validated on structured 2D data, MNIST, and a dedicated sampler study.
AI.conclusion: The new framework overcomes limitations of global averaging in score-based methods, providing a robust, mathematically sound approach with practical benefits for generative modeling and Langevin sampling.

title: PairSAE: Mechanistic Interpretability from Pair Representations in Protein Co-Folding
comment: Accepted at the Machine Learning in Structural Biology (MLSB) 2025 workshop
summary:
Foundation models for structural biology have achieved remarkable performance in predicting biomolecular structure and show promise for the design of proteins and small molecules. Yet understanding which internal features drive their outputs remains challenging. Standard sparse autoencoders (SAEs), effective on transformer-style sequence embeddings, do not transfer cleanly to pairformer-like architectures: naively operating on pairwise representations yields a quadratic blow-up of features and obscures concepts distributed jointly across sequence and pair representations.
  We introduce PairSAE, which summarizes pairwise tensors via an N-mode SVD into token-wise interaction roles, then uses a sparse autoencoder to learn a shared set of token-level features that decode into both sequence and pair representations. Evaluated on Boltz-2 activations for PLINDER protein-ligand complexes, PairSAE yields interpretable features that align with UniProt annotations and predict Boltz-2 affinity values. These results indicate that PairSAE links the latent space of foundation models for structural biology to interpretable structural concepts, clarifying what the model "knows" while avoiding pairformer-induced pitfalls that limit conventional SAEs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PairSAE, a method to make internal features of structural biology models interpretable by addressing challenges in pairwise representations.
AI.motivation: To address the difficulty of understanding internal features and outputs of foundation models for structural biology, particularly within pairwise representations in pairformer-like architectures.
AI.method: PairSAE summarizes pairwise tensors via an N-mode SVD, maps token-wise interaction roles, and then applies a sparse autoencoder to extract token-level features that decode into sequence and pair representations.
AI.result: PairSAE was evaluated on PLINDER protein-ligand data and demonstrated interpretable features that align with UniProt annotations, with predictive capability for Boltz-2 affinity values.
AI.conclusion: PairSAE overcomes limitations of standard sparse autoencoders for pairformer architectures by linking the latent space of structural biology models to interpretable structural concepts.

title: FoggyTrust: Robust Federated Learning with Hierarchical Trust Networks
summary: Byzantine-robust federated learning seeks to protect distributed model training from malicious or corrupted clients without requiring access to their private data. FLTrust addresses this challenge by introducing a trusted server-side root dataset that assigns trust scores to client updates for more robust aggregation. In this work, we propose FOGGYTRUST, a hierarchical extension of FLTrust that localizes trust computation to fog nodes, allowing the framework to better handle globally heterogeneous data while preserving robustness within locally homogeneous client groups. We further show that this two-level architecture can simultaneously address distribution mismatch in trust estimation and client drift across groups by combining local trust-based aggregation with heterogeneity-aware global optimizers such as FedAdam and SCAFFOLD. Across benchmark datasets, FOGGYTRUST achieves its strongest gains on more challenging heterogeneous settings, particularly on CIFAR-10 under Krum and Trim attacks, where it achieves an over 50% improvement over FLTrust. We also test FOGGYTRUST in a real-world safari dataset to show the promise of hierarchical trust networks for robust federated learning in socially impactful, safety-critical settings such as distributed wildlife monitoring.

AI.tldr: FOGGYTRUST extends FLTrust for robust federated learning by introducing a hierarchical trust computation at fog nodes, achieving over 50% improvement in heterogeneous, adversarial settings.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to enhance the robustness of federated learning systems against malicious or corrupted clients while addressing challenges arising from globally heterogeneous data.
AI.method: FOGGYTRUST employs a hierarchical approach where trust computation is localized to fog nodes. It combines local trust-based aggregation with heterogeneity-aware global optimization techniques like FedAdam and SCAFFOLD.
AI.result: FOGGYTRUST significantly outperforms FLTrust, achieving over 50% improvement in challenging heterogeneous environments, especially under attack scenarios (e.g., Krum and Trim attacks). It has also been validated in real-world applications like wildlife monitoring.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical trust networks, as implemented in FOGGYTRUST, offer a robust and effective solution for federated learning in settings with both data and trust heterogeneity, making it applicable to real-world, safety-critical domains.

title: Developmental approach reveals the statistical learning of Neural Language Models: Transformers generalize from the most abstract statistical patterns
comment: 10 pages, 7 figures, oral presentation at Interdisciplinary Advances in Statistical Learning
summary: In this study, we use a developmental approach to investigate the statistical learning and mental representation of neural language models (NLM). A series of Generative Transformer models are trained on a synthetic grammar. The model states are saved at multiple stages in the course of training. Through analyzing how the internal representations of these models change in the developmental path, we found that NLMs acquire the most abstract global statistical knowledge at the beginning of learning and later acquire the relatively local statistical dependencies. This learning path contains many over-generalizations from the very beginning and these over-generalizations are gradually constrained in the later stage of learning. Based on this observation, we propose a new framework to explain the statistical learning and language cognition of NLMs.

AI.tldr: This study explores statistical learning and mental representations in Neural Language Models (NLMs) by analyzing their developmental learning paths using Generative Transformer models.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to understand how NLMs internally represent and acquire linguistic statistical knowledge and how their learning progresses.
AI.method: The study trains Generative Transformer models on synthetic grammar while saving their states across multiple training stages to analyze how internal representations evolve over time.
AI.result: It was found that NLMs first acquire global statistical dependencies before local ones and make extensive over-generalizations early on, which are later corrected.
AI.conclusion: A new explanatory framework is proposed for understanding statistical learning and cognitive processes in NLMs based on observed learning patterns.

title: Towards Evaluation of Implicit Software World Models in Coding LLMs
comment: Accepted to DL4Code workshop at ICML 2026
summary: Software engineering, whether performed by humans or by AI agents, requires reasoning about how software behaves. We call the internal model that supports such reasoning the software world model, and view current code-execution benchmarks as covering one well-studied slice of it -- control flow. In this paper, we take a step toward a broader evaluation by shifting the observable axis to execution resources: alongside test outcome and exception class, we predict peak memory, wall-clock time, and ranked profiler outputs at method and line granularity. We use SWE-bench Verified as the source of data to hold the test close to real-world software engineering tasks. All tested models, frontier ones included, show modest performance and brittle behaviour, suggesting a notable lack of understanding of how software is executed, as opposed to how its source code is written.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses a new approach to software world modeling by evaluating execution resources such as memory and time, instead of just the control flow areas that are traditionally studied.
AI.motivation: With the advent of AI agents in software engineering, there's a need for models to better understand how software executes, beyond analyzing source code.
AI.method: The study uses SWE-bench Verified as the dataset to test memory usage, execution time, and profiler outputs at granular levels.
AI.result: Current frontier models show modest performance and lack depth in understanding software execution, suggesting gaps in their capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Models need significant improvement in understanding software execution behavior for future AI-driven software engineering tasks.

title: Learning to Throw: Agile and Accurate Cable-Suspended Payload Delivery with a Quadrotor
summary: Quadrotors offer the agility needed to rapidly transport suspended payloads during time-critical applications, including search-and-rescue and medical delivery. While suspended-payload transport and traversal for these missions are well studied, the highly dynamic targeted release of the payload remains comparatively underexplored. State-of-the-art approaches typically rely on model-based trajectory optimization and tracking; however, these methods often yield sub-optimal performance due to conservative feasibility constraints, tracking errors, and the inherent difficulty of analytically modeling flexible rope dynamics. To overcome these limitations, we propose a hybrid simulation framework that couples a high-fidelity analytical quadrotor model with a physics solver for complex rope and payload interactions. By exchanging forces between the two domains at every step, we obtain a physically accurate simulation of the suspended-payload system. Leveraging this environment, we train a deep reinforcement learning (RL) policy that executes agile, accurate payload throws to designated targets. Deployed zero-shot on hardware, our RL policy pushes the boundary of the agility-accuracy trade-off, outperforming the model-based baseline by reducing the landing error by up to 50% and the throw duration by up to 30%. Ablation studies confirm that the coupled simulation is the key enabler of these gains. We further show that the same pipeline trains a policy driven by visual observations rather than an explicit state estimate, achieving accuracy comparable to that of the state-based policy. To accelerate future research in dynamic aerial manipulation, we open-source the simulator to the community upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a hybrid simulation framework integrating analytical quadrotor and physics models to train reinforcement learning policies for dynamic payload release, outperforming existing methods in accuracy and speed.
AI.motivation: Suspended payload transport by quadrotors is crucial in scenarios like search-and-rescue, but dynamic and precise payload release remains underexplored due to model limitations and tracking errors.
AI.method: The authors developed a simulation combining a high-fidelity quadrotor model with a physics solver for rope-payload dynamics, enabling deep reinforcement learning methods for agile payload throws.
AI.result: The RL policy achieved up to 50% improvement in landing error and 30% faster throw durations compared to model-based baselines. Visual-based policies also matched state-based performance.
AI.conclusion: The coupled simulation framework enhances performance in dynamic aerial manipulation, pushing agility-accuracy trade-offs, and its open-source availability promises to accelerate research in the field.

title: DysLexLens: A Low-Resource LLM Framework for Analysing Dyslexic Learners Insights from Online Forums
summary: Dyslexic learners increasingly use artificial intelligence (AI) tools to support reading, writing, organisation, and study-related tasks. However, their lived experiences with these tools remain largely underexamined. This paper proposes DysLexLens, a low-resource LLM framework, designed to analyse dyslexic learners experience with AI through online forum discussions. DysLexLens is designed as an end-to-end, evidence-traceable architecture which transforms noisy social media posts into a dictionary-driven corpora, provides knowledge-graph (KG)-based question reasoning, generates verifiable query responses, and enables response evaluation through quantitative and human-grounded assessment. DysLexLens has four key features. First, it employs a dictionary-driven filtering method to construct a more focused Reddit corpus on dyslexia and AI, filtering out noisy and weakly related posts to improve the relevance of data collected from low-resource forum contexts. Second, it integrates LLM-assisted semantic analysis with KG-based query reasoning to uncover meaningful patterns. Third, it has quantitative evaluation metrics (RAGAS and Query Robustness) to measure LLM-generated response performance. Fourth, it provides structured qualitative validation guidelines for assessing response quality, with a specific focus on hallucination and evidence alignment. We demonstrate the effectiveness of DysLexLens using dyslexia-related Reddit forum data and 30 questions. The results show its potential generalisability to other low-resource forum data contexts. DysLexLens, sample data, questions and evaluation results are available at Github to support reproducibility.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces DysLexLens, a framework for analyzing dyslexic learners' experiences with AI tools using a structured, evidence-focused approach applied on Reddit forum posts.
AI.motivation: To investigate how dyslexic learners are engaging with and experiencing AI tools, as this is an underexplored area despite the growing importance of these tools in educational support.
AI.method: The proposed framework employs dictionary-driven filtering to focus on dyslexia-related posts, integrates LLM-based semantic analysis with knowledge-graph reasoning, and evaluates AI-generated query answers using metrics like RAGAS and qualitative validation measures.
AI.result: DysLexLens effectively identifies user experiences from Reddit data, providing structured insights and demonstrating potential generalizability in analyzing low-resource forum data.
AI.conclusion: DysLexLens enhances understanding of dyslexic learners' interactions with AI, offering a scalable and reproducible solution that may be applied in other contexts to improve AI-driven educational tools.

title: DMV-Bench: Diagnosing Long-Horizon Multimodal Agents' Visual Memory with Incidental Cue Injection
summary: Research on agent memory has matured rapidly, but almost entirely on the text side: few existing benchmarks ask, in an interactive environment, when an agent genuinely needs to remember what it saw rather than what it could write down. We introduce DMV-Bench (Code: https://github.com/yyyujintang/DMV-Bench), the first interactive benchmark for multimodal-agent visual memory. DMV-Bench is built on a controlled home-furnishing e-commerce catalogue of 1,000 product variants in which a text-leakage contract keeps the discriminative signal of each task in the pixels alone. Across a chain of autonomous shopping sessions, every visited product image carries a unique, pre-rendered incidental cue, and the agent is later asked to recall a particular cued product and navigate to its URL. Inspired by dual-coding theory, we propose DualMem, a memory architecture that maintains a visual and a verbal code in parallel. On DMV-Bench, DualMem outperforms a caption baseline and three recent multimodal agent-memory systems at every chain length J in {5, 10, 15, 50} on both Gemini 2.5 Flash and Qwen2.5-VL-7B, with the lead surviving controls for memory-bank size and encoding-position bias, and an asymmetric dual-coding regime in which vision carries the cue end-to-end while the verbal channel plays a smaller query-grounding role.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces DMV-Bench, the first interactive benchmark for evaluating multimodal-agent visual memory, and proposes DualMem, a novel dual-coding memory architecture that outperforms existing systems in visual memory tasks.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to create a benchmark that evaluates agents in multimodal, interactive environments where remembering visual information is essential, addressing the lack of such frameworks in existing research.
AI.method: DMV-Bench is built using a controlled product catalog where tasks rely entirely on visual signals. The authors also propose DualMem, a memory architecture using both visual and verbal codes to enhance memory performance.
AI.result: DualMem consistently outperformed other multimodal memory systems on DMV-Bench across various chain lengths and memory challenges, validating its effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: DualMem, with its dual-coding approach, demonstrates superior performance in multimodal visual memory tasks, making DMV-Bench a valuable tool for future research in this area.

title: Surprises in Proper Positive-Only Learning
summary: Binary classification from positive-only samples is a variant of PAC learning in which the learner receives i.i.d. samples from the positive region of an unknown target concept, but is evaluated under the original distribution (which places mass on both positive and negative regions). This model dates back to Natarajan [1987, STOC], and the characterization of improper learning is well-known -- it even appears in textbooks. The characterization of proper positive-only learning, however, has long remained open. In this work, we revisit and settle this question: a concept class is properly learnable from positive-only samples if and only if it has finite VC dimension and satisfies a new combinatorial condition, which we call uniform exterior separability. Together with several separation results, this characterization reveals a surprisingly rich landscape that differs sharply from standard PAC learning: proper and improper learning are separated, randomized and deterministic proper learning are separated, there are classes for which no ERM is a learner, and finite VC dimension does not suffice even for non-uniform learning. Along the way, we introduce new combinatorial dimensions that we believe can be of broader interest in learning theory.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses binary classification from positive-only samples, settling a long-standing open question by introducing the concept of uniform exterior separability.
AI.motivation: To resolve the open question of proper learnability from positive-only samples and understand its relationship with standard PAC learning.
AI.method: The paper defines a new combinatorial condition called uniform exterior separability, establishes a characterization for proper learning based on this condition, and provides separation results and new combinatorial dimensions for the analysis.
AI.result: The study reveals a distinct landscape for proper positive-only learning, including separations between proper and improper learning, randomized and deterministic learning, and the insufficiency of traditional metrics like VC dimension for this scenario.
AI.conclusion: Proper positive-only learning is determined by having finite VC dimension and satisfying uniform exterior separability, which diverges significantly from classical PAC learning, offering new insights and tools for learning theory.

title: Learning in Markovian bandits with non-observable states and constrained decision epochs
summary: This paper studies the problem of regret minimization in Markovian bandits with \emph{non-observable states} and possibly \emph{constrained} decision epochs. The focus is restricted to a ``pure'' regret benchmark, that compares the performance of the learning algorithm to the best \emph{pure policy} which -- akin to optimal policies of stochastic bandits -- picks the optimal arm from start to finish without ever switching. We introduce a generalization of rested Markovian bandits, \emph{self-degrading Markovian bandits}, for which pure policies are always asymptotically optimal.We show that without prior knowledge on the underlying bandit, the regret of algorithms that switch arms rarely necessarily scales super-logarithmically for every bandit, i.e., as $ω(\log(T))$, where $T$ is the learning horizon. Despite the unreachability of the logarithmic regime, we design UCB-NOM, an optimistic algorithm inspired by UCB, of which the regret is nearly logarithmic. Lastly, we show that given prior knowledge on the Markovian bandit in the form of a bound on the bias functions of its arm, a proper instantiation of UCB-NOM achieves $O(\log(T))$ regret. We further show that this prior knowledge allows for a $O(\sqrt{T \log(T)})$ worst-case regret bound for UCB-NOM. Notably, our regret bounds do not depend on the number of states of the underlying Markov chains. Our findings suggest that the non-observability of states is a mild inconvenience in self-degrading Markovian bandits.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses regret minimization in non-observable state Markovian bandits, proposes a novel bandit model, and introduces the UCB-NOM algorithm with promising regret bounds.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of regret minimization in Markovian bandits where states are non-observable and decision epochs may be constrained.
AI.method: Introduced the concept of self-degrading Markovian bandits and designed UCB-NOM, a regret-minimizing algorithm inspired by UCB, which operates effectively without state observability.
AI.result: The study shows super-logarithmic regret in the absence of bandit prior knowledge but achieves nearly logarithmic regret with UCB-NOM. With prior knowledge of bias bounds, it achieves $O(\log(T))$ regret and a worst-case bound of $O(\sqrt{T \log(T)})$.
AI.conclusion: The findings suggest that state non-observability is a manageable challenge in self-degrading Markovian bandits, and UCB-NOM provides strong regret minimization performance with appropriate prior knowledge.

title: P-ARC: Exploiting Subproblem Independence for Parallel Multi-Robot Motion Planning
summary: This paper presents Parallel ARC (P-ARC), a parallel variant of the Adaptive Robot Coordination (ARC) approach to multi-robot motion planning (MRMP). P-ARC proposes a parallel variant for each of the three main stages in ARC: initial individual solutions, conflict detection, and conflict resolution, exploiting the independence created by ARC's decomposition of the MRMP problem. Additionally, we employ an OR-parallel multi-start strategy to both ARC and P-ARC, creating a hybrid parallel strategy OR-P-ARC. We evaluate the impact of the different parallel strategies for ARC using a set of scaling 2D mobile and planar manipulator scenarios with up to 128 robots to control for conflicts and work distribution across the stages of ARC. Additionally, we demonstrate planning time speedups approaching 4X over the sequential version for large Panda multi-manipulator teams in real-world inspired scenarios when deploying 16 CPU cores.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces P-ARC, a parallelized approach to Adaptive Robot Coordination (ARC) for multi-robot motion planning, achieving up to 4x speedup over sequential solutions using 16 CPU cores.
AI.motivation: To address the computational complexity and inefficiency in multi-robot motion planning (MRMP) by developing a parallelized version of the ARC method.
AI.method: The authors parallelized the three main stages of ARC (initial solutions, conflict detection, and resolution) and applied an OR-parallel multi-start hybrid strategy (OR-P-ARC) for efficiency.
AI.result: P-ARC showed significant planning time improvements, including nearly 4x speedups compared to sequential ARC when implemented on large robot teams with 16 CPU cores.
AI.conclusion: Parallelizing ARC leads to substantial computational gains in MRMP, demonstrating its scalability and practicality for real-world multi-robot teams.

title: Supersede: Diagnosing and Training the Memory-Update Gap in LLM Agents
comment: 11 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Code, environment, model, and dataset: https://github.com/Vrin-cloud/supersede
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents operate over long, multi-session interactions in which facts change: a user moves, a price updates, a plan is revised. Acting correctly requires using the current value of a fact and discarding values that have been superseded. We isolate this ability on real conversational data and show that it is a distinct, unsolved failure. On the knowledge-update subset of LongMemEval, replacing an agent's full context with a bounded, self-maintained memory drops accuracy from 92% to 77% even on a frontier model (gpt-5.4), a gap that is statistically significant (paired McNemar p<0.005) and persists across model scale while full-context accuracy saturates near 92%. The bottleneck is therefore memory maintenance, not comprehension, and is not closed by a stronger model. We then ask whether this is merely an undersized memory, and find it is not: as the conversation grows 24x, accuracy falls further (from 68% to 28%), and granting the agent proportionally more memory yields no detectable recovery (28% to 28%, n=25). The failure scales with the length of the conversation, not the compression ratio. We release Supersede, an open reinforcement-learning environment (on the verifiers / prime-rl stack) that turns this measurement into a training signal: agents are rewarded for answering from the current value and penalized for stale ones. Finally, we close the loop and show the gap is trainable: GRPO fine-tuning a small open model (Qwen2.5-3B) on this environment nearly doubles its held-out supersession accuracy on real, unseen conversations (9.0% to 16.7%, a single run), along a monotonic checkpoint curve indicating the learned policy, not the harness, carries the gain. To our knowledge this is the first trainable environment whose reward targets temporal fact-currency, and the first evidence the supersession gap can be trained down, not only measured.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the failure of language model agents in maintaining effective memory across long conversational contexts and introduces 'Supersede,' an open reinforcement-learning environment to train models to address this gap.
AI.motivation: To address the critical issue of language model agents struggling with memory maintenance in long, multi-session conversations where facts change over time.
AI.method: The study benchmarked models on the knowledge-update subset of LongMemEval, analyzed performance gaps, scaled memory size, and introduced 'Supersede,' a training environment that rewards agents for using current facts and penalizes reliance on outdated ones.
AI.result: Experiments showed that increasing memory size could not improve accuracy in long conversations. However, training with 'Supersede' nearly doubled the supersession accuracy of a smaller language model.
AI.conclusion: Memory maintenance in long conversations is a bottleneck for language models, but this gap can be mitigated through proper training in specialized environments like Supersede.

title: Speculative Refinement: A Hybrid Autoregressive Diffusion Decoding Strategy and Its Behavior Across Benchmarks
summary: How should we evaluate generation systems that combine autoregressive (AR) and diffusion decoding? We study this question through Speculative Refinement (SpecRef), a training-free hybrid method that warm-starts a masked diffusion language model from an AR draft using entropy-guided selective masking. Evaluating SpecRef across six benchmarks (HumanEval, MBPP, GSM8K, BBH, ARC-Challenge, HellaSwag) with three distinct evaluation protocols (execution-based pass@1, exact-match, log-likelihood scoring), we surface several findings relevant beyond our specific system: (1) code benchmarks conflate structural discovery with logical correctness: providing a syntactic scaffold lifts accuracy from near zero to over 20% without changing the model, indicating that much of the baseline failure is structural; (2) a refinement tension phenomenon where multi-stage correction degrades already-correct tokens, exposing benchmark saturation ceilings invisible to single-model evaluation; (3) log-likelihood and generative evaluation produce different model rankings for the same model pair, suggesting they measure different capabilities; (4) standard Python post-processing silently breaks code evaluation for non-AR generators. These observations apply to any multi-stage or non-autoregressive generation pipeline and point toward more diagnostic evaluation practices.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates Speculative Refinement (SpecRef), a blend of autoregressive and diffusion decoding for language models, across six benchmarks and surfaces findings about evaluation practices.
AI.motivation: To explore how to effectively evaluate and diagnose hybrid generation systems like SpecRef that combine autoregressive and diffusion decoding.
AI.method: The study uses SpecRef, a training-free hybrid approach for language model decoding, across six benchmarks and three evaluation protocols, analyzing performance under diverse conditions.
AI.result: Key findings include: syntactic scaffolding significantly boosts accuracy in structural tasks, multi-stage refinement reveals limitations, different evaluation metrics yield varying model rankings, and standard Python post-processing adversely affects non-AR models.
AI.conclusion: Findings highlight critical challenges in current evaluation practices for hybrid generation systems, urging more diagnostic and diverse methodologies for proper capability assessment.

title: Physics-Guided Robotic Radiation Source Localization along Arbitrary Measurement Paths in Unstructured Environments
summary: Using robots to estimate the location of the radiation source is an effective way to improve efficiency and safety. Existing methods focus on planning the robot's path to achieve precise estimation, typically approaching the source. However, approaching the source increases the risk of radiation damage to a robot. In addition, a path-planning algorithm designed solely for radiation source localization (RSL) limits the flexibility of missions that deploy robots into radioactive environments. This study presents an automation framework for robotic RSL that leverages a physics-informed machine learning (PIML) model to precisely estimate the source location, regardless of measurement paths, in unknown environments. Physics-inspired model tensors have been designed for PIML to handle attenuated gamma-ray flux signals from unknown obstacles, and multiple models are computed in parallel to improve the robustness and precision of the RSL.
